dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 5 for a breathing difficulty at an apartment in [CITY].
Hello ma'am, my name is Fred and I'm an EMT. What made you call 911 today?
I've been having trouble breathing, especially when walking.
Can you tell me your name, please?
Yes. My name is Ruth.
Ma'am, what country are we in today?
We are in the United States.
What city are we in, ma'am?
[CITY], [STATE].
What date is it today?
It's July 13th.
Would you like to tell me what started the situation today?
I was just walking around my apartment and started having shortness of breath.
When did the shortness of breath start exactly?
It began suddenly yesterday morning around 8 AM.
Have you noticed anything that makes it better or worse?
It gets worse when I walk or if I exert myself in any way.
Has this ever happened before, ma'am?
No, this seems new.
Do you remember when you last had something to eat or drink?
I don't know when my last meal was.
Can you tell me what started the situation this afternoon?
Ruth was getting up to walk around in her apartment when her breath became labored and she called 911.
On a scale from 0 to 10, 10 being the worst pain you've ever had, can you describe your discomfort?
It's not like regular pain, but I would say around a 3 - it's uncomfortable.
Can you describe the feeling - sharp, dull, tight?
Just hard to breathe, no pain.
Has this trouble breathing moved anywhere else in your body like your arm or chest?
No it's only when I move.
Has the trouble breathing remained the same or gotten worse over the day?
It's the same. I don't feel better or worse.
Ma'am, will you allow us to do a quick examination of your airway?
Yeah, you can.
Your airway is clear and you're breathing adequately. I will now listen to your chest.
Resp: 20 breaths a minute, regular depth, equal chest rise and fall.
Lungs clear and equal bilaterally.
I'm going to lightly press here on your wrist to feel your pulse. Pulse is 101 and strong.
BP is 143/69. That's good.
Your skin looks normal, pink, warm, and dry. No pallor, rashes, or edema noted. Strong radial pulse present.
Ruth, can I shine a light in your eyes to check your pupils?
Please go ahead.
Pupils are reactive and equal bilaterally. You seem alert and aware, oriented to person, place, and time.
To check mental status-can you raise one arm and hold it out for me?
Sure. (She does so.)
No arm drift observed. Can you smile for me and squeeze both of my fingers at the same time?
Here you go. (She responds well.)
No facial droop noted, your speech is clear without slurring. Any headache or vertigo today?
No, just breathlessness.
I'm going to inspect your ears, nose, and throat now. Is that okay?
Alright go ahead.
No drainage or obstruction noted. No signs of tooth or gum involvement. Healthy appearance now noted.
Have you had any cough or fever recently?
No, I haven't had a cough or fever. I've been fine otherwise.
Have you ever had any episodes where you felt lightheaded or like you might faint, especially with low blood sugar?
No, I haven't felt anything like that today - or at any time really.
Ruth, I need to obtain a 12-lead ECG for a more complete look. Is that okay with you?
Sure.
SpO2 is 98%. That's good oxygen saturation.
Blood glucose is 160 mg/dL. Blood sugar is within normal limits.
EKG shows atrial fibrillation with premature ventricular contractions. No signs of STEMI noted.
Based on the EKG findings and your shortness of breath, we are following the respiratory distress protocol.
I'm going to begin a four-lead heart monitor for us to track your progress.
Next, I'll take a 12-lead ECG to give a complete picture of your heart function.
Pulse remains 101 and regular with no drop in oxygenation. BP still 143/69.
Ruth, though you feel okay currently, considering your shortness of breath and heart condition, we recommend transport to the hospital.
I already arranged with my aide to wait for dialysis tomorrow. I don't think I need transport tonight.
I understand, but I must explain the potential risks of not going in-like heart failure or worsening condition.
I understand the risks. Still, I feel fine and will see my PCP tomorrow.
We respect your right to refuse. Are you sure you do not want to go to the hospital?
Yes, I'm sure.
We will document your refusal and provide detailed records for your physician care.
She is stable now and has her home caretaker with her.
You stay alert and aware with no changes in your breathing, correct?
Yes, I'm fine now.
All EKG and vitals stable and compliant with your decision.
We will notify your PCP of the call and your conditions tonight.
Did you experience any cough, nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea recently?
No, nothing like that - even no diarrhea.
Have you had fever or chest discomfort or headache recently, ma'am?
No fever, chest pain, or headache - nothing like that today at all.
Have you had any feelings of dizziness or low blood sugar recently?
No, I've been alert and aware the whole time without any dizziness or lightheadedness.
We did not see any pallor, rashes, or signs of skin breakdown. No edema or abnormal skin turgor noted now.
You are alert, oriented to person, place, time, and event - A&Ox4. No forehead sweating, sudden weakness, or balance changes reported.
No signs of infection, nasal congestion, or oral sores. Mouth and throat look normal with no oral signs of issues now noted.
Final ECG results remain consistent with atrial fibrillation and occasional PVCs. No STEMI signs now observed during the testing process.
Pulse remains 101, BP 143/69, and SpO2 98% - still stable within normal limits.
Have you had any new pain, cough, or difficulty breathing since the exam started?
No, symptoms haven't changed since things first began yesterday morning around 8 AM as I described earlier.
You reported no signs of STEMI, stroke deficits, or any other life-threatening conditions at this time or even now according to this final assessment.
We have noted your refusal and will ensure the emergency physician is aware overall of what we've documented and seen tonight on this call.
As time passes, if symptoms change, chest pain develops, or more difficulty breathing arises, call 911 immediately, Ruth understands that clearly now.
Please stay in touch with your PCP, and ensure your home health aide is informed about this today's shortness of breath incident account and decision today.
Today's visit is complete, we'll have documentation sent to your provider upon arrival at the office for final review and response by them tomorrow morning.
Thank you again, and please keep us updated if anything changes in the next 24 hours regarding your status now that we are done here.
Again, hope your dialysis is scheduled for tomorrow, and we will reach out tomorrow morning for follow-up again.
Thank you for trusting our team, Ruth. We are always available whenever you or your CNA might need us again in the future!
If shortness of breath increases or chest discomfort appears, call 911 immediately as we have discussed plenty already today with you now on file.
We always recommend early evaluation for shortness of breath, especially for those on dialysis like yourself, Ruth as your history shows and we are aware of your status, too.
Dialysis dependency and hypertension are factors your PCP must understand to manage your condition moving forward after today's incident of shortness of breath when walking here in apartment today.

We make no further recommendations for EMS transport at this time and will leave you in the care of your aide and with current stable vitals today for now on record and report from this EMT call today, is complete clean refusal after full assessment.
Take care, Ruth. We wish you a safe evening with your aide right here with you now as we have ensured.

If you have any concerns later, feel free to reach out to your home care agency directly, they are on-site now and can communicate throughout the dialysis day tomorrow morning as you wait for dialysis today being missed as you mentioned earlier unaided and uninterruptedly today, so be sure to stay in touch with your team tomorrow as well as today things finalize here now with our EMT unit on scene and with this vehicle based documents and data complete.

Thanks for your cooperation and trust. We are glad you are stable now after our initial assessment and final checks without need for EMS transport or any longer-time assessment or Primary or Secondary measures here today with our team.

Pain is 0, you have no chest pain, no cough, no fever, no headache, no nausea, vomiting, or diarrhea - any of that says was helped now as you were ruled out twenty steps and twenty measures taken in total with full exam within the EMT protocol.
